1: The case of the man who has his father's wife (gynaika tou patros): Roman law (Lex Iulia de adulteriis, 18 BCE) prohibited sexual relations between a stepson and his stepmother; the Mishnah listed such relationships as severe sexual prohibitions (m. Yevamot 1 Corinthians 1:1). Paul's shock ('not even among pagans') indicates the offense violated both Jewish law and Greco-Roman legal convention. The Corinthians' boasting about this case (pephysiomenoi, you are arrogant, 1 Corinthians 5:2) may reflect a 'progressive' wing that interpreted Christian freedom (eleutheria) as transcendence of bodily ethics — a proto-Gnostic pneumatism that Paul vigorously opposes.
2: The community's boastful tolerance of the incest case (pephysiomenoi este, you are puffed up) reflects the Corinthians' valuation of sophrosyne in the sense of self-mastery, not communal moral accountability. In Greco-Roman voluntary associations (collegia), internal discipline over members' private sexual conduct was generally not practiced — associations were social-network organizations, not moral communities. The Corinthians may be applying this association-model to the church, treating personal sexual conduct as outside communal jurisdiction.
3: Paul's judicial action 'in spirit' while 'absent in body' invokes the concept of legal representation by letter, known in Greco-Roman practice — the absent party could authorize action through written mandate. The apostle acting through his written authority is analogous to the Roman legal concept of the mandatum (power of attorney). Paul's 'already judged' (kekrika, perfect tense) indicates a decision made and communicated through correspondence, a recognized form of formal judgment in the ancient world.
4: The assembly convened 'in the name of the Lord Jesus' with Paul's spirit present — the gathering language echoes Jewish community deliberation. In Second Temple Judaism, communal courts (beit din) convened to rule on cases of communal discipline; the Qumran community had elaborate rules for expulsion from the yahad (1QS 6-7). Paul's procedure here is analogous: a formal communal assembly pronounces a judicial verdict in the presence of divine authority, following recognizable patterns of Jewish communal jurisdiction adapted for the Gentile church.
5: The expulsion formula 'deliver to Satan for the destruction of the flesh' has parallels in both Jewish and Hellenistic contexts. In the Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS 1 Corinthians 2:5-9), the community's curse-formula devoted the wicked to Satan's dominion outside the covenant community. In Greco-Roman associations, expulsion (ekklēsia vote) placed the offender back in the social sphere dominated by honor-competition and spiritual peril. The formula assumes Satan's domain is outside the protected community — expulsion removes the offender from the church's zone of covenantal protection, a disciplinary measure meant to produce repentance, not final destruction.
6: The leaven metaphor ('a little leaven leavens the whole lump') was a common ancient proverb for the spread of corrupting influence. In Second Temple Judaism, leaven was associated with moral impurity — the Passover purge of leaven (Exodus 12:15-20) was ritually and metaphorically loaded. The Essenes regarded moral impurity as contagious within the community. Paul applies this Jewish purity logic to the Corinthian community's tolerance of the incest case: the community's corporate holiness is compromised by the uncorrected impurity within it.
7: Paul's Passover typology ('Christ our Passover has been sacrificed') draws on the foundational Jewish festival commemorating the Exodus from Egypt. The Passover lamb (Exodus 12:1-13) was sacrificed to protect Israel from the destroying angel. The metaphorical application was established in Second Temple Judaism: the Passover was interpreted as the prototype of God's eschatological redemption (Isaiah 43:16-19; 1 Corinthians 52:11-12). Paul's identification of Christ as the Passover lamb (not simply a Passover analogy) grounds the ethical imperative in the festival's temporal structure: the sacrifice has happened, now the festival of unleavened bread must begin.
8: The 'festival' (heortazomen, let us celebrate) picks up the Passover-festival metaphor: the sacrifice of the lamb was followed by the week-long festival of unleavened bread (Exodus 12:15-20; 1 Corinthians 13:6-7), during which all leaven was purged from the household and only unleavened bread was eaten. The Jewish household examination for leaven (bedikat chametz) before Passover was a formal religious duty. Paul translates this domestic purity rite into the community's expulsion of the sexually immoral member: the community is the Passover household that must be purged of its leaven before the festival can begin.
9: Paul references a prior letter ('I wrote to you in my letter') — a now-lost Pauline correspondence ('1 Corinthians' is actually the second extant letter). The Corinthians had apparently misunderstood the prior letter's injunction to avoid the immoral: they thought it meant avoiding all immoral people in the world, not only immoral members within the community. This misunderstanding reveals the practical challenge of Paul's community-ethics: the church existed in a pagan social environment where avoiding all contact with immoral people would require social withdrawal impossible in a Roman city.
10: The Corinthians' social world — the kosmos (world) Paul references — was densely populated with the behaviors he lists: pornoi (sexually immoral), pleonektai (greedy), harpages (swindlers), eidōlolatrēs (idolaters). Commercial Corinth, with its two harbors and cosmopolitan population, had an especially active economy of vice. Prostitution (porneia) was widespread and legally unregulated; commercial exploitation (pleonexia) was endemic in the Mediterranean trading economy. Complete social separation from such a world was not practicable.
11: The vice list for community discipline (1 Corinthians 5:11) includes both sexual and financial offenses, along with religious ones (idolater) and social ones (reviler, drunkard). The prohibition of table fellowship (synesthiein, eating together) was the most socially significant sanction available in the ancient world: meals were the primary social bonding mechanism, and exclusion from the common table was exclusion from the community's social existence. Jewish communities practiced table-separation to maintain purity; Paul applies this socially powerful tool to communal discipline.
12: Paul's distinction between judging insiders and outsiders (tous exō / tous esō) reflects the self-governance principle of the ancient association: the thiasos or collegium governed its own members' conduct but did not claim jurisdiction over non-members. Jewish communities similarly maintained internal courts (beit din) with jurisdiction over community members while recognizing Roman courts' jurisdiction over external matters. Paul invokes this principle: the church's communal discipline is legitimate within its own community; it has no mandate to police the wider world, whose judgment belongs to God.
13: The expulsion command ('purge the evil person from among you') cites Deuteronomy's communal-purification formula (Deuteronomy 17:7; 1 Corinthians 19:19; 1 Corinthians 22:21; 1 Corinthians 24:7) — each occurrence concerns a serious communal transgression requiring the community's purging to maintain its covenant status. Paul's use of Deuteronomic expulsion language signals that he regards the church as a covenant community with obligations analogous to those of Israel under the Torah. The community's holiness is not merely individual but corporate: the presence of unrepentant sin threatens the community's covenant integrity as a whole.
